speaker: interviewer
text: It is September 8, 1994. Interviewing survivor Colonel Jim Hayes. Interviewer is Mary Rothschild in Calabasas, and we're doing it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is September the 8th, 1994. We are in Los Angeles interviewing Colonel James Hayes. My name is Mary Rothschild. Please, tell us your full name, your date of birth, and location.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is James Henry Hayes. I was born in September the 14th, 1916, in Constantinople during World War I. My father was an American sailor and my mother was Greek. I was educated in Philadelphia. I attended the military academy-- the United States Military Academy from 1938 till 1942. And in May of 1942, I was commissioned as a second lieutenant in the regular Army. I immediately proceeded to Fort Benning, Georgia, where I took a three-month course in infantry basics. And then I was assigned in September of 1944 to the 80th Division located in Tullahoma, Tennessee. When I arrived at the division, I was assigned to the 317th Infantry and became a platoon leader in Company G of the 317th Infantry. In December of 1942, I became company commander of Company G. And this was my introduction, so to speak, as well to the existence of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were in Europe at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At the time, we were still in Tullahoma, Tennessee. And in December of 1942, one of my soldiers came to me-- I will not tell you his name for reasons which will become obvious later-- and he said that he had to speak to somebody. Now, it turned out that this soldier, who was an enlistee, rather than being inducted into the service, was a German Jew. And he told me that he had to tell somebody about the experience he had had prior to coming to the United States. At that time, he described to me a concentration camp in which he had been located. He described to me the mass killings that were going on. Such things as masturbation squads, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] forced intercourse with women, all sorts of bestial kinds of things. This was a little bit difficult for me to accept at the time, because I was 25 years old, recently graduated, only three months or four months before from the Military Academy. But I realized that there was something here of importance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took this soldier to my Division G2. The assistant division G2 was himself Jewish. And I turned this soldier over to him, so that at least as early as December of 1942, somebody in the official chain knew of the existence of-- of concentration camps, at least within the military chain of command.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we, as a division, went to Europe in June of 1945. And we landed in England sometime in mid June, late June. The date is not really important. And we went to Europe in early August and landed on Omaha Beach. Now, I think maybe I should just give you a brief description of what we did. We were in the-- in the great breakthrough and the pincer movement at Avranches, which destroyed the German armies that had been defending the-- the beaches. The result of that pincer movement was a mass killing, most of which occurred around Avranches. We proceeded from there across France in what was really a very rapid movement until we got to the Meuse River. At the Meuse River, we encountered the Germans very, very briefly, but they had been unable to establish a defense. So we broke across the Meuse River very, very rapidly and went to the Moselle River, where we arrived perhaps the 6th of September, 7th of September, something of that sort, 1944. In the initial attempt to cross that river, the division was ordered to execute what was known as a hasty crossing. And in the process, we suffered very severe casualties, because the German defense of the Meuse, while not very strong, was sufficient to permit them time to bring heavy forces up to the Moselle River. So when we got to the Moselle River, we ran into a thoroughly entrenched German army, and the fighting was very, very intense. To make a long story short, we crossed the Moselle River, perhaps the 11th, 12th of September, somewhere in there. The dates are not important. And from the-- from that date until the end of October of 1944, there was a tremendous battle in which the 317th Infantry in which I served suffered something on the order of 3,000 enlisted men casualties and 188, 190 officer casualties, which in effect meant that the regiment that had started on Omaha Beach had effectively disappeared. From that battle, which essentially ended in November with the capture of Metz by my division plus others, we then went south and were preparing to cross through the Siegfried Line. That was to have been in December, but it was interrupted by the Battle of the Bulge. We went north to the Battle of the Bulge, and my division participated in the relief of Bastogne and the subsequent defeat of the German effort that had led to the big bulge that gave the battle its name. Now, I have to add that during this dash across France and prior to these big battles, we were introduced to some of the bestiality of which the Germans were capable. For instance--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. To get back to the original point that I was making, we were introduced to some of the bestiality of which the Germans were capable. And while I think of it, we landed in Omaha Beach in August of '44, not '45. The war was over by '45. And the place where we ran into this first example of German brutality was a little town whose name escapes me at the moment. But it was at the base of a hill called Montsec. Apremont was the name of the town. And the Germans whom we had been chasing assembled all the men in the town, including male children and male babies, and executed them. So when my regiment arrived at Apremont, we were greeted by a group of wailing and weeping women and a burning town. This was our first introduction to some of the bestiality the Germans were capable of. In any event, after the-- the Battle of the Bulge, the war, in essence, turned very rapidly in our favor, and we very quickly made our way to the Rhine River. And my regiment crossed the Rhine River at Mainz on March the 26th, 1945. We did this in assault boats. We suffered some very severe casualties in so doing. But when we got on the other side, the German resistance virtually collapsed. And in short order, we captured the city of Wiesbaden. Now, from Wiesbaden, we turned north and went to the city of Kassel, which at the time was a manufacturing city with a population of perhaps 100,000, 150,000 people. And one of the things that was really strange about this is as we were fighting for Kassel, we kept knocking out German tanks, and they kept reappearing. And it turned out that there was a tank factory in the city of Kassel, and there was a German tank school in the city of Erfurt, which was to the east. And as we were knocking the tanks out, they were bringing crews in and bringing tanks off the assembly line and attacking us. But in any event, in about 10 days of fighting, we forced the Germans to surrender. And we were in the process of mopping up the city, and my colonel sent me through to make a reconnaissance to find a place where we could put the regiment for that night. And this was my first actual encounter with victims of the Holocaust. What happened is, as I was driving through-- and this was in the afternoon sometime, sometime in April of 1945-- I noticed a group of people standing that were dressed in these black and white uniforms. And I stopped my jeep and walked up. And there was a German SS officer, I thought, or else a policeman, whatever, all dressed up. His boots were immaculately polished, and he was perspiring profusely, which is very unusual, because April is quite chilly in Germany. I walked into the building, and when I got in there, the sight that greeted me was my first view of the Holocaust. This was a building that was perhaps-- the part that I saw-- the size of my entire house. And it had these wooden bunks that we've all seen so often in perhaps four or five layers. And in these bunks, on the floors, and scattered around the room were people dead, dying, getting ready to die, in a total-- total stupor. Some dressed, some undressed. And the stench was just awful. And this is the part of pictures that doesn't come through, because what you encounter in these places is the-- the stench of the dying who are defecating and urinating on themselves. In addition to that, you're running into the stench of people who have already died and begun to decay. In any event, I quickly realized that this was a big medical problem that we had on our hands. And while I could not stay very long because, in combat, you just don't have time to stay anywhere, I went out to my jeep and called our medical doctor, Dr. [INAUDIBLE], a major who was the regimental surgeon, and told him that we had this medical problem. And I briefly described what I had encountered. I must say that before I did this, as I walked out, this German chap came up to me and wanted to shake hands with me. And I-- at the point, I almost shot him. I was so irritated and-- and horrified by what I had seen. But what I did do was I indicated with a wave of my hand that those men that were still able-bodied and strong could have this man. And I am absolutely certain that I condemned him to death, for which I have absolutely no remorse. And I'm sure they tore him limb from limb when I left, but I just turned on my heels went back to my jeep and called the regiment. Now, from Kassel, we turned eastward. And the first city in which we, again, encountered the victims of the Holocaust was in Gotha. And Gotha was a German city where the camp known as Ohrdruf was-- was located somewhere near Gotha. I personally did not see Gotha, but our units liberated it. And I have some pictures that were given to me by people that were there, which we can look at later. Now, from Gotha, we went to Weimar, which, as you know, is the center of German culture and has been much described in the literature. And it was here that we liberated Buchenwald. Now, as a technicality, we did not technically liberate Buchenwald. Because by the time we arrived at Buchenwald, the Germans knew that the jig was up, so to speak, and the inmates, in effect, took control of the camp. But research by our division-- historians and other people in our division-- indicate that the first American soldiers into the camp was a patrol from Company 8A of the 317th Infantry, which was my regiment. Now, the following day I remember very, very vividly was-- that night. That night was the night that President Roosevelt died. And the following day, I remember very vividly Sam Williams, who commanded our Second Battalion, came to me and he said, you ought to go and look at this. So I went. And I went into the Buchenwald place, and here again, the horror was unspeakable. There were like acres of dead people, because Buchenwald was a- a true camp where they-- where they really tried to kill everybody and eliminate everybody that they could. And the horrors were unspeakable. I was later to find out-- although, again, I did not stay very long at Buchenwald, because we had to move eastward-- I was later find out that at Buchenwald, we had liberated things like gloves made out of the skin of men's hands. They-- they had taken people, cut their hands off, tanned, deboned it, and then tanned it and made these into supposedly human leather. There were baskets full of-- of gold teeth. There were baskets and baskets full of hair, which they had taken out that they were going to apparently use to stuff mattresses and things like that. They-- they had soap that had been made out of human oils, which the camp personnel had extracted by boiling human beings and then saponifying the oil. And then, I remember in particular-- and I saw this later at the war crimes trial-- we had captured a-- a box that was about this big and about this wide. And it was filled with alcohol. And as you looked at it from one side, there was a human head in it in profile, and the neck had been cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here was the head. And then when you turned it around like this, what you saw were all of the cavities of-- of the head. So that somebody had very carefully sawn this head in half, and then mounted it in this thing for some kind of medical experiment. I will tell you later about some of the experiments that were conducted.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we then continued our march east. And by this time, the German army essentially was defeated. And the concentration camp system was beginning to fall apart, and the inmates were beginning to crowd the roads and became refugees. And they were moving in both directions. There were refugees from places like Belgium, France, Luxembourg, as well as people from the-- from the Eastern nations, as well as Russian soldiers. There were many Russian soldiers in these camps. So anyway, we then began to get reports virtually every day of new camps being liberated, of new groups of people being found. But my next real encounter with them was after leaving Weimar, a few days later-- you know, I don't quite remember the exact days-- we liberated the city of Jena. And my contact with Jena was rather extensive because at this point, my colonel had, again, sent me on a mission to find a way through this city. And this city was now overrun by people that had escaped from the various concentration camps in the vicinity, as well as people trying to move east and get away. And I remember when I came to the city, my first sight, oddly enough, was a German man all dressed up in spats and I guess you'd call it a day coat. And he was chopping stakes off a dead horse that was in the road, because everybody was starving, and there was no food. Everything had broken down. And as I left him to go in, I reached the town square. And in the town square, there was a riot going on. There were hundreds-- hundreds of displaced people in here who were trying to break into the food stores that were in the square, where there was, of course, no food. And what they were doing was they were creating a tremendous block to the road which our regiment needed to get through in order to continue our movement to the east. So I remember at the time that I had a Tommy gun with me, which is a submachine gun. And I fired across the head of the crowd. And that broke-- everybody scattered and left. But as I continued on, I came to another crowd. And this was-- this was women. What it was was a storehouse that the Germans had set up for clothes. And these women were in the house-- in the storehouse trying to get rid of the rags that they were wearing, and they were trying to put new clothes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, again, this was a blockage in the road. And I remember saying to the women-- there was a sign that said [SPEAKING GERMAN], which says whoever plunders will be killed. And their reaction to this was, so what, you know? So anyway, I told them, well, you an put on what you could wear. And I remember that they undressed in front of me and started to put clothes on, because the agreement was they could-- they could go away with whatever they were wearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in due course, I finally got them to move on. Went on and through the place, and I came to a wine cellar that had been bombed the previous day, presumably. And all of the barrels were in the open. And there were, again, hundreds of displaced people drinking wine and getting drunk. And I remember at the time, there was a Frenchman who seemed to be the ringleader. Fortunately, I spoke French at the time. And I told him, I said, look, you know, I can understand all this, but go. We got to get-- we got to get through here to continue the war. And he defied me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember at the time taking my Tommy gun and I bip-bipped at his feet. And he looked at me astounded as if, you know, I'm your ally. What are you doing this for? And I said, I want to get through. Go away. [SPEAKING FRENCH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he, again, defied me. So I bip-bipped him again with a couple of shots. And he finally came to the conclusion that maybe I was going to shoot him, and he moved away. And when he moved away, the crowd broke up. And so we went through. Now, we passed through Jena, and we came to Gera. Yes, it was Gera. And we got on the Autobahn that led to Dresden. And at some point, it was going to intersect the Autobahn that went to-- to Berlin. Here, we ran into another really horrible sight. The Autobahn was a natural route for people to walk and try to get east, because it was all well-grated, and all that sort of stuff. And what had happened is the German unit which we were following-- or chasing, basically-- had seen these people. And there were literally thousands of them walking down the Autobahn. And as they came by, they machine gunned them. And there was just 40 or 50 armored vehicles, presumably. Well, I don't know the exact number. But assuming-- assuming that there was a fair number of vehicles, they all turned their guns on these people. And so as we came by, there were hundreds-- hundreds of people-- women, children, men. Without regard to anything, they had just simply machine gunned them, because these people had these black and white uniforms on. Shortly after that, we got to a German town called Oberleimbach. Oberleimbach. The reason I remember it is because the GIs very quickly dubbed it 'Oh My Lame Back,' which was a favorite expression with the GIs. And at that point-- this is now very late April, perhaps early May-- we received orders to evacuate, which was a good part of East Germany. There is little doubt that at that time we could have gone to Berlin and gotten there before the Russians. But in any event, the politics of the situation where such that we gave up all of the land that we had captured, turned around, and went south. And we went across the-- I think it was the Danube River. It was Donau, which is Danube. And we crossed into Austria at a city called Brunau, B-R-U-N-A-U. And when we got into Austria, we once again began to run into displaced people who were trying now to get away from the Russians. And the-- these people, I now found out, had as much hatred for the Russians as they did for the Germans. Because by this time, many of the Russian divisions were-- were manned by Mongolian troops who were incredibly cruel. And there was a great deal of raping and pillaging and all that sort of stuff going on, because the-- the Russian army apparently did not have the same standards at the time as we did, or else they didn't care. Because the people that they were doing this to were the Germans, whom they, probably with good reason, despised.
